g4-multdiv.title: Multiplication & Division Made Easy! 🧮

sections.heading: What is Multiplication?
sections.body: Multiplication is just a fast way of adding the same number over and over! If you have 4 bags with 3 apples in each bag, instead of adding 3+3+3+3, you can multiply: 4 × 3 = 12. The × symbol means 'groups of.' So 4 × 3 means '4 groups of 3.'

sections.heading: What is Division?
sections.body: Division is the opposite of multiplication — it's about sharing equally! If you have 12 cookies and want to share them with 3 friends, how many does each friend get? 12 ÷ 3 = 4. Each friend gets 4 cookies! The ÷ symbol means 'shared equally among.'

sections.heading: The Two Are Best Friends!
sections.body: Multiplication and division are inverse operations — they undo each other, just like addition and subtraction. If 6 × 4 = 24, then 24 ÷ 4 = 6 and 24 ÷ 6 = 4. Understanding this relationship makes both operations much easier!

sections.heading: Mental Math Tricks
sections.body: You can break big multiplication problems into smaller ones! For 7 × 8, if you know 7 × 4 = 28, then double it: 28 + 28 = 56. For 9s facts, use your fingers or remember that the digits always add up to 9 (like 9×7=63, and 6+3=9).

sections.heading: Place Value Helpers
sections.body: When multiplying by 10, just add a zero! 24 × 10 = 240. When multiplying by 100, add two zeros: 24 × 100 = 2,400. This is because each place value is 10 times bigger than the one before it.

tips: Practice your times tables up to 10×10 until they become automatic — it makes everything else easier!
Always check your work by doing the reverse operation (multiply to check division, divide to check multiplication).
Draw pictures! Arrays (rows and columns of dots) help you SEE what multiplication means.

g4-fractions.title: Fractions — Your First Look at Parts of a Whole 🍕

sections.heading: What is a Fraction?
sections.body: A fraction shows a PART of a whole. If you cut a pizza into 8 equal slices and eat 3, you ate 3/8 (three-eighths) of the pizza. The top number (numerator) tells HOW MANY parts you have. The bottom number (denominator) tells HOW MANY equal parts the whole is divided into.

sections.heading: Numerator and Denominator
sections.body: Think of the fraction bar (the line) as meaning 'out of.' So 3/4 means '3 out of 4 equal parts.' The denominator can NEVER be zero — you can't divide something into zero parts!

sections.heading: Types of Fractions
sections.body: Proper fractions: The numerator is smaller than the denominator (like 3/4) — these are less than 1 whole. Improper fractions: The numerator is bigger (like 7/4) — these are more than 1 whole. Mixed numbers: A whole number plus a fraction (like 1 3/4). Unit fractions: The numerator is 1 (like 1/3).

sections.heading: Comparing Fractions
sections.body: With unit fractions (all have 1 on top), the BIGGER the denominator, the SMALLER the piece! 1/10 is smaller than 1/4 because the whole is cut into more pieces. When denominators are the same, just compare the numerators — 3/8 is bigger than 1/8.

sections.heading: Fractions in Real Life
sections.body: Fractions are everywhere! A quarter of an hour is 15 minutes (1/4 of 60). Recipes use fractions: 1/2 cup of milk. Your video game progress bar might show 3/4 complete. Fractions help us describe amounts that aren't whole numbers.

tips: Always make sure the parts are EQUAL when talking about fractions — a fraction only makes sense when the whole is divided into equal pieces.
Use food like pizza, cake, or chocolate bars to practice fractions — it's the tastiest way to learn!
Remember: denominator = DOWN (both start with D). Numerator is up top!

g4-decimals.title: Decimals — Numbers Smaller Than One 🔢

sections.heading: What are Decimals?
sections.body: Decimals are another way to write fractions! Instead of writing 3/10, we can write 0.3. The decimal point separates whole numbers from parts of a whole. Everything to the right of the decimal point is less than one.

sections.heading: Tenths and Hundredths
sections.body: The first place after the decimal is tenths (1/10). The second place is hundredths (1/100). So 0.3 means '3 tenths' — the same as 3/10. And 0.25 means '25 hundredths' — the same as 25/100. Think of money: $0.25 is 25 cents, which is 25/100 of a dollar!

sections.heading: Reading Decimals
sections.body: Read decimals by saying the place value. 0.7 is 'seven tenths.' 0.45 is 'forty-five hundredths.' 2.3 is 'two and three tenths.' Saying them this way helps you understand what the decimal really means instead of just saying 'point seven.'

sections.heading: Decimals and Fractions Are Friends
sections.body: Decimals and fractions are just two ways of writing the same thing! 0.5 = 1/2, 0.25 = 1/4, 0.75 = 3/4, 0.1 = 1/10. Learning to switch between them helps you solve problems in whichever way is easier.

sections.heading: Comparing Decimals
sections.body: To compare decimals, line up the decimal points and compare digits from left to right. Is 0.45 bigger than 0.5? No! 0.5 = 0.50, and 50 hundredths > 45 hundredths. Adding zeros to the right of the last decimal digit doesn't change the value — 0.5 = 0.50 = 0.500.

tips: Money is the best way to practice decimals! Count your change and think about tenths and hundredths of a dollar.
Always line up the decimal points when comparing or adding decimals — this keeps the place values aligned.
A zero at the end of a decimal (like 0.50) doesn't change its value, but a zero between the decimal point and other digits (like 0.05) is very important!

g4-patterns.title: Patterns & Relations — Finding the Hidden Rules 🔍

sections.heading: What are Number Patterns?
sections.body: A pattern is a sequence that follows a rule. In math, we look at how numbers change from one to the next. For example: 3, 6, 9, 12, 15... The rule is 'add 3 each time.' Finding the rule helps you predict what comes next!

sections.heading: Input/Output Tables
sections.body: An input/output table is like a machine — you put a number IN, the rule transforms it, and a new number comes OUT! If the rule is 'multiply by 2 and add 1,' then input 3 → output 7 (because 3×2+1=7). Your job is to figure out the hidden rule by looking at the pairs.

sections.heading: How to Find a Pattern Rule
sections.body: Step 1: Look at how the numbers change. Are they getting bigger (addition or multiplication) or smaller (subtraction or division)? Step 2: Check if the change is always the same amount. Step 3: Test your rule on ALL the pairs to make sure it works every time. If it fails on even one pair, it's not the right rule!

sections.heading: Growing Shape Patterns
sections.body: Patterns aren't just numbers — shapes can grow in patterns too! A row of squares might grow by adding 2 squares each time. Count how many in step 1, step 2, step 3... and find the number pattern hidden in the shapes.

sections.heading: Why Patterns Matter
sections.body: Patterns are everywhere in math and in life! The times tables follow patterns. Days of the week repeat in a pattern. Even nature has patterns — flower petals, pinecones, and snowflakes all follow mathematical rules. Understanding patterns helps you solve problems and make predictions.

tips: Always test your pattern rule on at least 3 pairs to make sure it's correct.
Draw a table to organize input and output numbers — it makes the pattern much easier to see.
Look for TWO operations: some patterns use both multiplication and addition (like '×2 then +1').

g4-measurement.title: Measurement — How Big, How Heavy, How Much? 📏

sections.heading: Length — How Long?
sections.body: Length tells you how long something is. In Canada, we use metric units: millimetres (mm) for tiny things, centimetres (cm) for small things, metres (m) for bigger things, and kilometres (km) for long distances. 10 mm = 1 cm. 100 cm = 1 m. 1,000 m = 1 km. A pencil is about 15 cm long. A soccer field is about 100 m long.

sections.heading: Mass — How Heavy?
sections.body: Mass (often called weight) tells you how heavy something is. We use grams (g) for light things and kilograms (kg) for heavier things. 1,000 g = 1 kg. A paperclip weighs about 1 gram. A textbook weighs about 1 kilogram. A car weighs about 1,000 kg!

sections.heading: Capacity — How Much Can It Hold?
sections.body: Capacity measures how much liquid a container can hold. We use millilitres (mL) for small amounts and litres (L) for larger amounts. 1,000 mL = 1 L. A teaspoon holds about 5 mL. A water bottle holds about 500 mL. A big milk jug holds 4 L.

sections.heading: Area — How Much Space?
sections.body: Area tells you how much flat space a shape covers. For a rectangle, multiply length × width. If a rug is 3 metres long and 2 metres wide, its area is 3 × 2 = 6 square metres (6 m²). Area is measured in square units — imagine how many 1 cm × 1 cm tiles would cover the shape.

sections.heading: Converting Between Units
sections.body: When you need to change from one unit to another, remember: to go from a BIG unit to a SMALL unit, MULTIPLY (because you need more small units). To go from SMALL to BIG, DIVIDE. For example: 3 m to cm = 3 × 100 = 300 cm. 500 cm to m = 500 ÷ 100 = 5 m.

tips: Always pick the right unit for what you're measuring — don't measure a pencil in kilometres!
When converting, remember the 'King Henry' trick: Kilo (1,000), Hecto (100), Deka (10), Base (1), Deci (0.1), Centi (0.01), Milli (0.001).
Check your answers by asking: 'Does this make sense?' A person cannot be 150 m tall!

g4-geometry2d.title: 2D Geometry — Flat Shapes All Around Us 📐

sections.heading: What is a Polygon?
sections.body: A polygon is a closed, flat shape made of straight lines. The word comes from Greek: 'poly' means many and 'gon' means angles. Triangles have 3 sides, quadrilaterals have 4 sides, pentagons have 5, hexagons have 6. The name tells you how many sides!

sections.heading: Triangles — 3-Sided Friends
sections.body: Triangles can be sorted by their SIDES: Equilateral (all 3 sides equal, all angles 60°), Isosceles (2 sides equal), Scalene (all sides different). They can also be sorted by their ANGLES: Acute (all angles < 90°), Right (one 90° angle), Obtuse (one angle > 90°). The sum of all three angles in ANY triangle is always exactly 180°!

sections.heading: Quadrilaterals — 4-Sided Shapes
sections.body: Quadrilaterals are shapes with 4 sides and 4 angles. The sum of their angles is always 360°. There are several types: Square (4 equal sides, 4 right angles), Rectangle (opposite sides equal, 4 right angles), Rhombus (4 equal sides, angles not necessarily 90°), Parallelogram (opposite sides parallel and equal), Trapezoid (exactly one pair of parallel sides). Every square is a rectangle, but not every rectangle is a square!

sections.heading: Angles — How Wide?
sections.body: An angle measures how much something turns. Right angle: exactly 90° (like the corner of a book). Acute angle: less than 90° (sharp and pointy). Obtuse angle: more than 90° but less than 180° (wide and open). Straight angle: exactly 180° (a straight line). A full circle is 360°.

sections.heading: Lines of Symmetry
sections.body: A line of symmetry divides a shape into two identical halves that are mirror images. A square has 4 lines of symmetry. A rectangle has 2. An equilateral triangle has 3. An isosceles triangle has 1. A scalene triangle has 0. Fold the shape in half — if both sides match perfectly, you found a line of symmetry!

tips: Draw shapes on grid paper to help you see if sides are equal and angles are right.
Remember: All squares ARE rectangles (they have 4 right angles), but not all rectangles are squares.
The sum of angles in a triangle is ALWAYS 180°. If you know two angles, subtract from 180° to find the third!

g4-data.title: Data & Graphs — Pictures That Tell Stories 📊

sections.heading: What is Data?
sections.body: Data is information you collect — like your classmates' favorite colors, how many books you read each month, or the types of pets people have. Once you collect data, you need a way to organize it so people can understand it at a glance. That's where graphs come in!

sections.heading: Bar Graphs
sections.body: A bar graph uses bars (rectangles) to show data. The taller the bar, the more there is of that category! Bars can go up (vertical) or sideways (horizontal). Every bar graph needs: a title, labeled axes (the X-axis across the bottom and Y-axis up the side), and bars that don't touch each other. Use a ruler to make bars the right height!

sections.heading: Pictographs
sections.body: A pictograph uses pictures or symbols instead of bars. Each picture represents a certain number of items — this is called the KEY. For example, one 🍎 might represent 5 apples. Pictographs are fun to look at and easy to understand, but they can be less precise than bar graphs when the numbers don't divide evenly.

sections.heading: Line Plots
sections.body: A line plot shows data along a number line. You put an X (or dot) above each value for each time it appears. Line plots are great for showing how data is spread out and for finding the most common value (the mode). They work well with smaller sets of numbers.

sections.heading: Reading Graphs
sections.body: When you read a graph, always: (1) Read the title to know what it's about. (2) Check the labels to understand what's being measured. (3) Look at the scale — how much each unit represents. (4) Compare the categories. Ask yourself: Which has the most? Which has the least? What's the difference between them?

tips: Always give your graph a clear title so people know what they're looking at.
Make sure your bars or pictures are evenly spaced and the scale is consistent.
When collecting data, use tally marks (|||| with the fifth mark crossing through) to count quickly and accurately.

g4-probability.title: Probability — What Are the Chances? 🎲

sections.heading: What is Probability?
sections.body: Probability is the chance that something will happen. We use special words to describe how likely an event is: CERTAIN (it will definitely happen, like the sun rising tomorrow), LIKELY (it will probably happen, like rain on a cloudy day), UNLIKELY (it probably won't happen, like finding a four-leaf clover), and IMPOSSIBLE (it can never happen, like a fish riding a bicycle).

sections.heading: Equally Likely Events
sections.body: Some events have the same chance of happening. When you flip a fair coin, heads and tails are EQUALLY LIKELY — each has a 1 out of 2 chance. When you roll a fair die, each number from 1 to 6 is equally likely — each has a 1 out of 6 chance. 'Fair' means nothing is rigged or biased!

sections.heading: How to Measure Probability
sections.body: We can write probability as a fraction: (favourable outcomes) ÷ (total possible outcomes). If you pick one marble from a bag with 3 red and 7 blue marbles (10 total), the probability of picking red is 3/10 and the probability of picking blue is 7/10. Since 7/10 is bigger than 3/10, picking blue is MORE LIKELY.

sections.heading: Probability Scale
sections.body: Think of probability like a number line from 0 to 1. Impossible = 0. Certain = 1. Equally likely = 1/2 (0.5). The closer to 0, the less likely. The closer to 1, the more likely. Likely events are > 1/2. Unlikely events are < 1/2.

sections.heading: Probability in Everyday Life
sections.body: Probability helps us make decisions every day! Weather forecasts use probability ('70% chance of rain'). Games use probability (what are my chances of rolling a 6?). Even sports use probability (likelihood of scoring from a certain position). Understanding probability helps you make smarter choices!

tips: Count the favourable outcomes (what you WANT to happen) and divide by the total possible outcomes.
A 'fair' game or experiment means every outcome has an equal chance — no cheating!
If you do an experiment many times, the results should get closer to the predicted probability. Try flipping a coin 50 times and see how close you get to 25 heads!
